categories.name: Battery
categories.recommended_action: Drop off at a certified e-waste collection point or retailer take-back bin
categories.disposal_text: Batteries contain toxic heavy metals and flammable electrolytes. Never place in general waste or recycling bins. Take to a designated battery drop-off point such as a retailer collection bin or authorized e-waste recycler.
safety_notes: Do not puncture, crush, or expose to heat
If swollen, place in a fireproof container and dispose of immediately
Keep away from metal objects to prevent short-circuit
Lithium batteries can cause fires if damaged — handle with care
categories.environmental_note: Lithium-ion batteries leach cobalt and nickel into soil and groundwater. Proper recycling recovers critical minerals and prevents toxic contamination.

categories.name: Charger / Power Adapter
categories.recommended_action: Donate if functional; recycle via e-waste drop-off if broken
categories.disposal_text: Chargers and power adapters contain copper wiring and flame-retardant plastics. Do not place in household recycling. Deposit at an e-waste collection point or retailer drop-box.
safety_notes: Do not use damaged or frayed chargers — risk of electric shock or fire
Unplug before inspecting or disposing
Do not expose to moisture
categories.environmental_note: Copper recovery from charger wiring significantly reduces the need for virgin copper mining, which is energy-intensive and ecologically damaging.

categories.name: Cable / Wire
categories.recommended_action: Recycle via e-waste drop-off or cable-specific collection bin
categories.disposal_text: Cables and cords contain recoverable copper and may have gold-plated connectors. While low-hazard, they should not go into general waste. Bundle and bring to an e-waste or scrap metal recycler.
safety_notes: Inspect for fraying before reuse
Do not incinerate — burning plastic insulation releases toxic fumes
categories.environmental_note: Copper is highly recyclable. Recovering copper from cables reduces the environmental footprint of mining and smelting new copper ore.

categories.name: Smartphone / Tablet
categories.recommended_action: Repair if possible; trade in or donate if functional; recycle via certified e-waste recycler
categories.disposal_text: Smartphones and tablets contain lithium batteries, rare earth metals, and hazardous flame retardants. Never dispose in general waste. Use a manufacturer take-back program, certified e-waste recycler, or retailer trade-in scheme.
safety_notes: Wipe personal data before disposal (factory reset)
Remove and separately recycle the SIM and memory cards
Do not attempt to remove swollen batteries yourself — take to a professional
Cracked screens may contain mercury in older LCD backlights
categories.environmental_note: Manufacturing a single smartphone generates approximately 70 kg of CO₂. Extending device life through repair or refurbishment is the single most impactful action a consumer can take.

categories.name: Earphones / Headphones
categories.recommended_action: Repair if possible; recycle via e-waste drop-off
categories.disposal_text: Earphones and headphones contain small lithium batteries (wireless models), copper wiring, and neodymium magnets in drivers. Do not bin — take to an e-waste collection point.
safety_notes: Wireless earbuds contain micro lithium cells — do not crush or puncture
Do not place in regular recycling bins
If battery is leaking, handle with gloves
categories.environmental_note: Neodymium, used in speaker drivers, is a rare earth metal with an environmentally intensive extraction process. Recycling helps reduce demand for new mining.

categories.name: Keyboard / Mouse
categories.recommended_action: Donate if functional; recycle via e-waste drop-off
categories.disposal_text: Keyboards and mice are low-hazard but should not go into general waste due to their mixed plastic and metal content. Take to an e-waste collection point or donate to a community reuse scheme.
safety_notes: Remove batteries from wireless devices before disposal
Do not place in household recycling
categories.environmental_note: The aluminum and steel components in keyboards and mice are highly recyclable and can be recovered with low energy input compared to primary production.

categories.name: Small Appliance
categories.recommended_action: Repair if possible; donate if functional; recycle via municipal e-waste collection
categories.disposal_text: Small appliances contain a mix of metals, plastics, and sometimes hazardous components such as heating elements and capacitors. Do not place in general waste. Take to a municipal e-waste collection event or authorized recycler.
safety_notes: Unplug completely and allow to cool before handling
Do not attempt to repair mains-connected appliances unless qualified
Microwaves contain high-voltage capacitors — professional disassembly only
categories.environmental_note: Small appliances are one of the fastest-growing e-waste streams globally. Repair and reuse are strongly preferred over recycling to maximise resource value.

categories.name: Laptop / Notebook
categories.recommended_action: Repair or upgrade if possible; donate or trade in if functional; recycle via certified e-waste recycler
categories.disposal_text: Laptops contain lithium batteries, rare earth metals, flame-retardant circuit boards, and mercury in older screens. Never dispose in general waste. Use a manufacturer take-back program or certified e-waste recycler. Ensure data is wiped before disposal.
safety_notes: Perform a secure data wipe or physical drive destruction before disposal
Do not remove the internal battery unless trained — risk of puncture and fire
Older LCD screens may contain mercury — do not break
Handle swollen batteries with extreme caution
categories.environmental_note: A laptop's manufacturing phase accounts for roughly 80% of its lifetime carbon footprint. Extending a laptop's useful life by even two years can halve its effective CO₂ per year of use.

categories.name: TV / Monitor
categories.recommended_action: Donate if functional; recycle via authorized e-waste recycler — do not place at kerbside
categories.disposal_text: Televisions and monitors contain hazardous materials including lead solder, mercury (LCD backlights), and flame-retardant plastics. CRT televisions contain significant amounts of lead in the glass. All screens must be taken to an authorized e-waste recycler — never placed in general waste or kerbside collection.
safety_notes: Do not break the screen — LCD panels may contain mercury
CRT screens contain up to 4 kg of lead — never smash or cut
Large screens require two people to move safely
Do not leave at unauthorized roadside dumps
categories.environmental_note: Indium, used in LCD and OLED displays, is one of the rarest elements on Earth. Recovering indium from screens is critical as natural reserves are projected to be depleted within decades.

categories.name: Printer / Scanner
categories.recommended_action: Donate if functional; recycle via manufacturer take-back or authorized e-waste recycler
categories.disposal_text: Printers contain ink or toner (which can be toxic), circuit boards, motors, and plastic casings. Remove and separately recycle any remaining ink or toner cartridges. Take the device to an authorized e-waste recycler or manufacturer take-back point.
safety_notes: Remove ink/toner cartridges before disposal and recycle them separately
Laser toner powder is a respiratory irritant — avoid inhaling
Do not place in general recycling
categories.environmental_note: Printer toner cartridges contain microplastics and chemical pigments. Many manufacturers offer dedicated cartridge take-back programmes — use them before disposing of the printer.

categories.name: Non-E-Waste Item
categories.disposal_text: This item does not qualify as e-waste. Please use your local municipal recycling or composting program.
safety_notes: No special handling required
categories.environmental_note: Diverting this item from landfill through standard recycling still helps reduce environmental impact.

disposal_channels.name: Authorized E-Waste Recycler
disposal_channels.description: Certified processors that safely dismantle and recover materials from e-waste using environmentally sound methods. They are licensed to handle hazardous components and ensure maximum material recovery.

disposal_channels.name: Manufacturer Take-Back Program
disposal_channels.description: Brand-run schemes operated by manufacturers such as Apple, Samsung, Dell, and HP for their own products. Programs often offer trade-in credits, free recycling, or certified data destruction.

disposal_channels.name: Retailer Drop-Box
disposal_channels.description: In-store collection bins available at electronics retailers and some supermarkets. Convenient for small items and batteries — no appointment needed.

disposal_channels.name: Municipal Collection Drive
disposal_channels.description: City or council-organized periodic collection events that accept a wide variety of household e-waste. Typically free of charge for residents and held at community centres or car parks.

disposal_channels.name: Repair Café / Local Shop
disposal_channels.description: Community repair workshops and local repair shops that fix electronics, extending device lifespan and keeping items out of the waste stream entirely. Often low-cost or donation-based.
